title: Intermediate-mass black holes: existence & formation
statement: Black holes are well established in two regimes: stellar-mass (~3-100 M_sun, from stellar collapse) and supermassive (~10^6-10^10 M_sun, in galactic nuclei). Intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs, ~100-10^5 M_sun) are the expected bridge, but observational confirmation is sparse and their formation channels uncertain. The open problem: do IMBHs exist as a genuine population, where (globular clusters, dwarf-galaxy nuclei, ultraluminous X-ray sources?), and how do they form (runaway stellar collisions, repeated mergers, leftover SMBH seeds)? Gravitational-wave events like GW190521 (a ~150 M_sun remnant) and dynamical/X-ray candidates are narrowing the gap.

originators.name: (community)
originators.note: no single originator; the 'missing link' framing emerged from cluster dynamics and ULX observations.

ranking.rationale: Existence/formation is an observational and astrophysical-dynamics question, actively narrowed by gravitational waves (GW190521), dynamics, and X-ray surveys; shallow fundamentally and progressing, so near the bottom.

key_ideas: Candidate sites: cores of globular and nuclear star clusters; nuclei of dwarf galaxies; some ultraluminous X-ray sources (ULXs).
Formation channels: runaway collisions of massive stars in dense clusters; hierarchical (repeated) mergers of stellar-mass black holes; surviving direct-collapse SMBH seeds.
GW190521 produced a ~150 M_sun remnant — the first fairly secure IMBH-scale object — and its components fall in/near the pair-instability gap (#16).
Dynamical mass measurements in clusters are contentious (alternatives: a dense subcluster of stellar remnants can mimic an IMBH).
An IMBH population would test SMBH seed scenarios (#12) and hierarchical-merger predictions.
Future facilities (LISA, ngVLA, next-gen X-ray) target the IMBH regime directly.

approaches.name: Gravitational-wave detection
approaches.summary: Identify IMBH-scale remnants and hierarchical mergers in LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA (and future LISA) data.

approaches.name: Dynamical / X-ray candidate searches
approaches.summary: Measure central masses in clusters and characterize ULXs and tidal-disruption events.

approaches.name: Cluster N-body formation models
approaches.summary: Simulate runaway collisions and repeated mergers to predict IMBH masses and rates.

papers.title: Intermediate-Mass Black Holes
papers.note: Definitive modern review (published Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 58, 257, 2020). Surveys dynamical and accretion searches for M_BH ~ 100-10^5 Msun; finds no solid globular-cluster detections and few constraints in the 100-10^4 Msun range. arXiv:1911.09678.

papers.title: Observational evidence for intermediate-mass black holes
papers.note: Review of IMBH formation scenarios and observational evidence in dwarf galaxies, globular clusters, and ULXs (Int. J. Mod. Phys. D 26, 1730021).

papers.title: Where are the Intermediate Mass Black Holes
papers.note: Astro2020 white paper summarizing IMBH detection challenges and the role of LISA.

papers.title: Fast-moving stars around an intermediate-mass black hole in omega Centauri
papers.note: Nature 631, 285. Seven fast-moving stars in the central 3 arcsec of omega Cen imply a firm lower mass limit of ~8,200 Msun — currently the strongest local IMBH case.

papers.title: Evidence for an Intermediate Mass Black Hole in Omega Centauri
papers.note: Early dynamical claim of a ~4x10^4 Msun central BH in omega Cen from a central velocity-dispersion rise (ApJ 676, 1008). Later contested; precursor to the 2024 fast-star result.

papers.title: Growing the Intermediate-mass Black Hole in Omega Centauri
papers.note: Monte Carlo N-body models grow a ~5x10^4 Msun IMBH from 500-5000 Msun seeds over 12 Gyr, reproducing omega Cen's fast-star population.

papers.title: No Evidence for Accretion around the Intermediate-mass Black Hole in Omega Centauri
papers.note: Ultradeep ATCA radio observations detect no emission, constraining the accretion efficiency of the proposed omega Cen IMBH to be exceptionally low.

papers.title: GW190521: A Binary Black Hole Merger with a Total Mass of 150 Msun
papers.note: Phys. Rev. Lett. 125, 101102. Merger of ~85 + ~66 Msun BHs producing a ~142 Msun remnant — the first directly observed IMBH-mass remnant from gravitational waves.

papers.title: Properties and astrophysical implications of the 150 Msun binary black hole merger GW190521
papers.note: Companion astrophysics paper (ApJ Lett. 900, L13) discussing the pair-instability mass gap, hierarchical and stellar-merger formation channels for the GW190521 source.

papers.title: Search for intermediate-mass black hole binaries in the third observing run of Advanced LIGO and Advanced Virgo
papers.note: Astron. Astrophys. 659, A84. Dedicated O3 search; no further IMBH binaries beyond GW190521; sets merger-rate upper limits (e.g. ~0.056 Gpc^-3 yr^-1 for 200 Msun).

papers.title: Search for gravitational radiation from intermediate mass black hole binaries in data from the second LIGO-Virgo joint science run
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 89, 122003. Early null search setting coalescence-rate upper limits on IMBH binaries (100-450 Msun) in initial LIGO/Virgo data.

papers.title: Detectability of intermediate-mass black holes in multiband gravitational wave astronomy
papers.note: Nature Astronomy 4, 260. Shows joint LIGO/LISA/next-gen detectors can survey IMBH binaries to cosmological redshifts; provides analytic detectability relations.

papers.title: Runaway collisions in young star clusters - II. Numerical results
papers.note: MNRAS 368, 141. Monte Carlo simulations establishing that core collapse within the massive-star lifetime drives runaway mergers forming a ~400-4000 Msun very massive star, candidate IMBH progenitor.

papers.title: Formation of intermediate-mass black holes through runaway collisions in the first star clusters
papers.note: MNRAS 472, 1677. Cosmological + N-body simulations: runaway collisions in first star clusters build central stars of ~400-1900 Msun collapsing to IMBHs.

papers.title: Intermediate-mass black holes from stellar mergers in young star clusters
papers.note: MNRAS 507, 5132. 10^4 cluster simulations: IMBHs up to ~438 Msun form efficiently via runaway stellar collisions (metallicity-dependent), rarely via BH-BH mergers due to low escape velocity.

papers.title: FROST-CLUSTERS III. Metallicity-dependent intermediate mass black hole formation by runaway collisions in dense star clusters
papers.note: Large suite of BIFROST cluster simulations mapping IMBH mass vs. cluster surface density and metallicity; suggests many local IMBHs form at z<1.5-3 rather than as early SMBH seeds.

papers.title: Binary Black Hole Mergers and Intermediate-mass Black Holes in Dense Star Clusters with Collisional Runaways
papers.note: AJ semi-analytic study of GW signals from IMBH-BH mergers following collisional runaways; assesses detectability by LISA, LIGO Voyager, ET, and Cosmic Explorer.

papers.title: The Formation of Intermediate-mass Black Holes in Galactic Nuclei
papers.note: ApJ Lett. 929, L22. Proposes IMBH growth to ~10^4 Msun via collisions between stellar-mass BHs and main-sequence stars in galactic nuclei.

papers.title: Impact of gas hardening on the population properties of hierarchical black hole mergers in active galactic nucleus disks
papers.note: Astron. Astrophys. 685, A51. Semi-analytic AGN-disk model: efficient gas hardening enables hierarchical mergers building IMBHs up to ~10^4 Msun.

papers.title: THE COOL ACCRETION DISK IN ESO 243-49 HLX-1: FURTHER EVIDENCE OF AN INTERMEDIATE-MASS BLACK HOLE
papers.note: ApJ 734, 111. Relativistic disk fits to HLX-1 (brightest ULX) place it firmly in the IMBH regime (~3000 Msun to 3x10^5 Msun).

papers.title: A REDSHIFT FOR THE INTERMEDIATE-MASS BLACK HOLE CANDIDATE HLX-1: CONFIRMATION OF ITS ASSOCIATION WITH THE GALAXY ESO 243-49
papers.note: ApJ Lett. 721, L102. Spectroscopic confirmation that HLX-1 lies in ESO 243-49, securing its extreme luminosity and IMBH (>500 Msun) interpretation.

papers.title: Mass, Spin, and Ultralight Boson Constraints from the Intermediate-mass Black Hole in the Tidal Disruption Event 3XMM J215022.4-055108
papers.note: ApJ 918, 46. Relativistic disk fitting of an off-nuclear TDE yields M ~ 1.75x10^4 Msun and a spin measurement — the first clear IMBH with a measured spin.

papers.title: An 85-s X-ray quasi-periodicity after a stellar tidal disruption by a candidate intermediate-mass black hole
papers.note: Nature Astronomy. QPO timing of TDE 3XMM J2150 jointly constrains mass (~1.0-1.6x10^4 Msun) and spin, demonstrating IMBH study via TDE X-ray timing.

papers.title: Hunting for massive black holes in dwarf galaxies
papers.note: Nature Astronomy 6, 26. Perspective on the growing population of massive/intermediate-mass black holes in dwarf galaxies and what they reveal about seed formation.

papers.title: First Detection of an Overmassive Black Hole Galaxy UHZ1: Evidence for Heavy Black Hole Seed Formation from Direct Collapse
papers.note: ApJ Lett. 960, L1. Chandra-JWST detection of a Compton-thick AGN at z~10.1 with M_BH ~ M_star, interpreted as a heavy (direct-collapse) seed candidate — bears on the high-mass end of the seed/IMBH problem.

physicists.name: M. Coleman Miller
physicists.contribution: Co-authored the foundational review framing the intermediate-mass black hole problem and developed formation/dynamics theory for IMBHs in dense clusters.

physicists.name: Simon Portegies Zwart
physicists.contribution: Originated the runaway stellar-collision channel for IMBH formation in young dense star clusters (Portegies Zwart & McMillan 2002, ApJ 576, 899).

physicists.name: Jenny E. Greene
physicists.contribution: Lead author of the definitive modern ARA&A review on IMBHs; pioneer in finding low-mass/active black holes in dwarf galaxies.

physicists.name: Amy E. Reines
physicists.contribution: Leads observational searches for massive black holes in dwarf galaxies, constraining the black hole occupation fraction and seeding models.

physicists.name: Mar Mezcua
physicists.contribution: Authored a widely-cited review of observational IMBH evidence; works on IMBH candidates in dwarf galaxies, ULXs, and globular clusters.

physicists.name: Nadine Neumayer
physicists.affiliation: Max Planck Institute for Astronomy, Heidelberg
physicists.contribution: Co-led the 2024 detection of fast-moving stars around the candidate IMBH in omega Centauri, the strongest local IMBH case.

physicists.name: Maximilian Haberle
physicists.affiliation: Max Planck Institute for Astronomy, Heidelberg
physicists.contribution: Lead author of the omega Centauri fast-moving-stars study (Nature 2024) establishing a firm >8,200 Msun lower mass limit.

physicists.name: Karl Gebhardt
physicists.contribution: Pioneered dynamical (velocity-dispersion) searches for central black holes in globular clusters including the early omega Centauri IMBH claim.

physicists.name: Michela Mapelli
physicists.contribution: Leads star-cluster and AGN-disk simulations of IMBH formation via runaway collisions and hierarchical mergers, connecting to gravitational-wave sources.

physicists.name: Priyamvada Natarajan
physicists.contribution: Developed heavy (direct-collapse) black hole seed theory and the overmassive-black-hole-galaxy prediction confirmed by the JWST/Chandra source UHZ1.

simulator_angle: Mass-agnostic for the GR renderer (an IMBH renders like any Kerr/Schwarzschild hole at the right mass); relevant mainly as the demographic bridge, not a new geometry.

metadata.coverage_note:
Assembled 25 verified, genuinely-relevant papers and 10 physicists for the IMBH existence/formation problem. Method: ran 11 Consensus MCP searches (mcp__plugin_bio-research_consensus__search) across varied sub-themes — IMBH evidence/reviews, runaway stellar collisions in clusters, GW190521 and LVK IMBH-binary searches, ULX HLX-1, omega Centauri dynamical and radio constraints, fast-moving-star detection, dwarf-galaxy massive BHs, TDE probes, AGN-disk hierarchical mergers, and direct-collapse/JWST seeds. 24 of the 25 papers carry a real Consensus/arXiv URL in identifier.url returned directly by search; the bibliographic 'note' fields add journal/year/volume detail. One canonical originator (Portegies Zwart & McMillan 2002, ApJ 576, 899) is cited only within a physicist contribution rather than as a separate entry because it was not surfaced by the search tool, so it is not counted among the 25 to respect the no-invention rule. Tiers reflect the open status: GW/EM detections and null searches are observational_constraint, cluster/cosmological simulations are numerical_approximation, and the early-universe seed connection remains an open question. No titles, authors, years, DOIs, arXiv ids, or URLs were invented; entries I could not verify were omitted rather than padded.</coverage_note>
</invoke>
